abstract:
The elemental composition of organic aerosol (OA) particles provides critical constraints on their sources, chemical evolution, and environmental impacts. The Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) is the standard instrument for measuring these properties. In this study, we re-evaluate AMS measurements of atomic oxygen-to-carbon (O:C), hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C), and organic mass-to-organic carbon (OM:OC) ratios, as well as the carbon oxidation state ($OS_C$), using a significantly expanded laboratory dataset of multifunctional oxidized organic standards.

We tested the performance of the "Aiken-Explicit" method—which utilizes all experimentally measured ion intensities—and found it reproduces known O:C and H:C ratios within 20% and 12%, respectively. However, the more common "Aiken-Ambient" method, which relies on empirical estimates for $H_2O^+$ and $CO^+$ to avoid atmospheric gas interferences, consistently underestimates these ratios, particularly for alcohols and simple diacids. A closer look at the high-resolution mass spectra reveals that the Aiken-Ambient method frequently fails to account for the full intensity of $CO^+$ and $H_2O^+$ fragments. By combining AMS with vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) ionization, we determined that these fragments are largely the result of dehydration and decarboxylation occurring on the instrument’s vaporizer, a process that remains efficient even at temperatures as low as 200 °C.

To address these systematic biases, we developed an "Improved-Ambient" elemental analysis method. By using specific ion fragments as markers for different molecular functionalities, this new approach reproduces O:C and H:C ratios of individual standards within 28% and 13% of their molecular values. The accuracy further improves when analyzing complex mixtures, which better represent real-world ambient OA. When applied to ambient datasets, the Improved-Ambient method yields O:C and H:C values that are 27% and 11% higher, respectively, than those produced by the original Aiken-Ambient method, leading to a 9% increase in estimated OM:OC ratios. While these findings suggest that ambient OA is more oxygenated than previously thought, the calculated $OS_C$ values remain remarkably consistent between methods. This confirms that $OS_C$ is a particularly robust metric for oxidation, as it remains unaffected by the hydration and dehydration processes occurring during analysis or in the atmosphere.
